is a solid material made of loose particles that is transported to a new location where it is deposited.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It occurs naturally and, through the processes of [weathering](/wiki/Weathering "Weathering") and [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), is broken down and subsequently [transported](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") by the action of wind, water, or ice or by the force of [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") acting on the particles. For example, [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") and [silt](/wiki/Silt "Silt") can be carried in [suspension](/wiki/Suspension_(chemistry) "Suspension (chemistry)") in river water and on reaching the sea bed deposited by [sedimentation](/wiki/Sedimentation "Sedimentation"); if buried, they may eventually become [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and [siltstone](/wiki/Siltstone "Siltstone") ([sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock")) through [lithification](/wiki/Lithification "Lithification").

**Sediment** is a solid material made of loose particles that is transported to a new location where it is deposited.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It occurs naturally and, through the processes of [weathering](/wiki/Weathering "Weathering") and [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), is broken down and subsequently [transported](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") by the action of wind, water, or ice or by the force of [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") acting on the particles. For example, [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") and [silt](/wiki/Silt "Silt") can be carried in [suspension](/wiki/Suspension_(chemistry) "Suspension (chemistry)") in river water and on reaching the sea bed deposited by [sedimentation](/wiki/Sedimentation "Sedimentation"); if buried, they may eventually become [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and [siltstone](/wiki/Siltstone "Siltstone") ([sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock")) through [lithification](/wiki/Lithification "Lithification").

Sediments are most often transported by water ([fluvial processes](/wiki/Fluvial_sediment_processes "Fluvial sediment processes")), but also wind ([aeolian processes](/wiki/Aeolian_processes "Aeolian processes")) and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier"). Beach sands and [river channel](/wiki/Stream_channel "Stream channel") deposits are examples of fluvial transport and [deposition](/wiki/Deposition_(geology) "Deposition (geology)"), though sediment also often settles out of slow-moving or standing water in lakes and oceans. Desert sand dunes and [loess](/wiki/Loess "Loess") are examples of aeolian transport and deposition. [Glacial](/wiki/Glacial "Glacial") [moraine](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine") deposits and [till](/wiki/Till "Till") are ice-transported sediments.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Sediment_off_the_Yucatan_Peninsula.jpg/250px-Sediment_off_the_Yucatan_Peninsula.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sediment_off_the_Yucatan_Peninsula.jpg)

Sediment can be classified based on its [grain size](/wiki/Particle_size_(grain_size) "Particle size (grain size)"), grain shape, and composition.

### Grain size

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Grain size")]

Main article: [Particle size (grain size)](/wiki/Particle_size_(grain_size) "Particle size (grain size)")

See also: [soil texture](/wiki/Soil_texture "Soil texture") and [Unified Soil Classification System](/wiki/Unified_Soil_Classification_System "Unified Soil Classification System")

Sediment size is measured on a log base 2 scale, called the "Phi" scale, which classifies particles by size from "colloid" to "boulder".

### Shape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Shape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Rounding_%26_sphericity_EN.svg/330px-Rounding_%26_sphericity_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rounding_%26_sphericity_EN.svg)

Schematic representation of difference in grain shape. Two parameters are shown: sphericity (vertical) and [rounding](/wiki/Roundness_(geology) "Roundness (geology)") (horizontal).

The shape of particles can be defined in terms of three parameters. The *form* is the overall shape of the particle, with common descriptions being spherical, platy, or rodlike. The *roundness* is a measure of how sharp grain corners are. This varies from well-rounded grains with smooth corners and edges to poorly rounded grains with sharp corners and edges. Finally, *surface texture* describes small-scale features such as scratches, pits, or ridges on the surface of the grain.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

#### Form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Form")]

See also: [Sphericity](/wiki/Sphericity "Sphericity")

Form (also called *sphericity*) is determined by measuring the size of the particle on its major axes. [William C. Krumbein](/wiki/William_C._Krumbein "William C. Krumbein") proposed formulas for converting these numbers to a single measure of form,[[3]](#cite_note-3) such as

:   ![{\displaystyle \psi _{l}={\sqrt[{3}]{\frac {D_{S}D_{I}}{D_{L}^{2}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/030809916d2dfd59f90138643c6790863b819b56)

where ![{\displaystyle D_{L}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e44b39c353d4634b76f550679dcc7aed38a0cd47), ![{\displaystyle D_{I}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b0f957251c6a6bc9a57d27380d6171478958e46d), and ![{\displaystyle D_{S}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec66bd07fe0649713f001c8a0af2fd5dca47c6d9) are the long, intermediate, and short axis lengths of the particle.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoggs2006582-4) The form ![{\displaystyle \psi _{l}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fa1966cd9561248abaf6cb27e59666a096ae1b9d) varies from 1 for a perfectly spherical particle to very small values for a platelike or rodlike particle.

An alternate measure was proposed by Sneed and Folk:[[5]](#cite_note-5)

:   ![{\displaystyle \psi _{p}={\sqrt[{3}]{\frac {D_{S}^{2}}{D_{L}D_{I}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bf2d62b6ef07c4ee45b9480de2a87ff9d6a8513f)

which, again, varies from 0 to 1 with increasing sphericity.

#### Roundness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Roundness")]

Main article: [Roundness (geology)](/wiki/Roundness_(geology) "Roundness (geology)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Rounding.gif/250px-Rounding.gif)](/wiki/File:Rounding.gif)

Comparison chart for evaluating roundness of sediment grains

Roundness describes how sharp the edges and corners of particle are. Complex mathematical formulas have been devised for its precise measurement, but these are difficult to apply, and most geologists estimate roundness from comparison charts. Common descriptive terms range from very angular to angular to subangular to subrounded to rounded to very rounded, with increasing degree of roundness.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoggs200666–67-6)

#### Surface texture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Surface texture")]

Surface texture describes the small-scale features of a grain, such as pits, fractures, ridges, and scratches. These are most commonly evaluated on [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz") grains, because these retain their surface markings for long periods of time. Surface texture varies from polished to frosted, and can reveal the history of transport of the grain; for example, frosted grains are particularly characteristic of [aeolian](/wiki/Aeolian_processes "Aeolian processes") sediments, transported by wind. Evaluation of these features often requires the use of a [scanning electron microscope](/wiki/Scanning_electron_microscope "Scanning electron microscope").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoggs200668–70-7)

### Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Composition")]

Composition of sediment can be measured in terms of:

* Parent [rock](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)") [lithology](/wiki/Lithology "Lithology")
* [Mineral](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") composition
* [Chemical](/wiki/Chemical "Chemical") make-up

This leads to an ambiguity in which [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") can be used as both a size-range and a composition (see [clay minerals](/wiki/Clay_mineral "Clay mineral")).

## Sediment transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sediment transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/StoneFormationInWater.jpg/250px-StoneFormationInWater.jpg)](/wiki/File:StoneFormationInWater.jpg)

Sediment builds up on human-made breakwaters because they reduce the speed of water flow, so the stream cannot carry as much sediment load.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Glacial_Transportation_and_Deposition.jpg/250px-Glacial_Transportation_and_Deposition.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glacial_Transportation_and_Deposition.jpg)

Glacial transport of boulders. These boulders will be deposited as the glacier retreats.

Main article: [Sediment transport](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport")

See also: [Rouse number](/wiki/Rouse_number "Rouse number")

Sediment is transported based on the strength of the flow that carries it and its own size, volume, density, and shape. Stronger flows will increase the lift and drag on the particle, causing it to rise, while larger or denser particles will be more likely to fall through the flow.

### Fluvial processes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Fluvial processes")]

This section is an excerpt from [Fluvial sediment processes](/wiki/Fluvial_sediment_processes "Fluvial sediment processes").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fluvial_sediment_processes&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Matanuska_River_8727.JPG/250px-Matanuska_River_8727.JPG)](/wiki/File:Matanuska_River_8727.JPG)

Deep, eroding [glaciofluvial deposits](/wiki/Glaciofluvial_deposit "Glaciofluvial deposit") alongside the [Matanuska River](/wiki/Matanuska_River "Matanuska River"), [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska")

In [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography") and [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [fluvial sediment processes](/wiki/Fluvial_sediment_processes "Fluvial sediment processes") or fluvial sediment transport are associated with [rivers](/wiki/River "River") and [streams](/wiki/Stream "Stream") and the [deposits](/wiki/Deposition_(geology) "Deposition (geology)") and [landforms](/wiki/Landform "Landform") created by sediments. It can result in the formation of [ripples](/wiki/Ripple_marks "Ripple marks") and [dunes](/wiki/Dune "Dune"), in [fractal](/wiki/Fractal "Fractal")-shaped patterns of erosion, in complex patterns of natural river systems, and in the development of [floodplains](/wiki/Floodplain "Floodplain") and the occurrence of [flash floods](/wiki/Flash_floods "Flash floods"). Sediment moved by water can be larger than sediment moved by air because water has both a higher [density](/wiki/Density "Density") and [viscosity](/wiki/Viscosity "Viscosity"). In typical rivers the largest carried sediment is of [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") and [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel") size, but larger floods can carry [cobbles](/wiki/Cobble_(geology) "Cobble (geology)") and even [boulders](/wiki/Boulders "Boulders").
When the stream or rivers are associated with [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier"), [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), or [ice caps](/wiki/Ice_cap "Ice cap"), the term *[glaciofluvial](/wiki/Glaciofluvial "Glaciofluvial")* or [*fluvioglacial*](/wiki/Fluvioglacial_landform "Fluvioglacial landform") is used, as in [periglacial](/wiki/Periglacial "Periglacial") flows and [glacial lake outburst floods](/wiki/Glacial_lake_outburst_flood "Glacial lake outburst flood").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Fluvial sediment processes include the [motion of sediment](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") and [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") or [deposition](/wiki/Deposition_(geology) "Deposition (geology)") on the [river bed](/wiki/River_bed "River bed").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Main article: [Aeolian processes](/wiki/Aeolian_processes "Aeolian processes")

Wind results in the transportation of fine sediment and the formation of sand dune fields and soils from airborne dust.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Glacial processes")]

### Mass balance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Mass balance")]

Main article: [Exner equation](/wiki/Exner_equation "Exner equation")

The overall balance between sediment in transport and sediment being deposited on the bed is given by the [Exner equation](/wiki/Exner_equation "Exner equation"). This expression states that the rate of increase in bed elevation due to deposition is proportional to the amount of sediment that falls out of the flow. This equation is important in that changes in the power of the flow change the ability of the flow to carry sediment, and this is reflected in the patterns of erosion and deposition observed throughout a stream. This can be localized, and simply due to small obstacles; examples are scour holes behind boulders, where flow accelerates, and deposition on the inside of [meander](/wiki/Meander "Meander") bends. Erosion and deposition can also be regional; erosion can occur due to [dam removal](/wiki/Dam_removal "Dam removal") and [base level](/wiki/Base_level "Base level") fall. Deposition can occur due to dam emplacement that causes the river to pool and deposit its entire load, or due to base level rise.

## Shores and shallow seas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Shores and shallow seas")]

See also: [Marine sediments](/wiki/Marine_sediments "Marine sediments") and [Coastal sediment transport](/wiki/Coastal_sediment_transport "Coastal sediment transport")

Seas, oceans, and lakes accumulate sediment over time. The sediment can consist of *terrigenous* material, which originates on land, but may be deposited in either terrestrial, marine, or lacustrine (lake) environments, or of sediments (often biological) originating in the body of water. Terrigenous material is often supplied by nearby rivers and streams or reworked [marine sediment](/wiki/Marine_sediment "Marine sediment") (e.g. [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand")). In the mid-ocean, the exoskeletons of dead organisms are primarily responsible for sediment accumulation.

Deposited sediments are the source of [sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock"), which can contain [fossils](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") of the inhabitants of the body of water that were, upon death, covered by accumulating sediment. Lake bed sediments that have not solidified into rock can be used to determine past [climatic](/wiki/Climate "Climate") conditions.

### Key marine depositional environments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Key marine depositional environments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/EolianiteLongIsland.JPG/250px-EolianiteLongIsland.JPG)](/wiki/File:EolianiteLongIsland.JPG)

[Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") [eolianite](/wiki/Eolianite "Eolianite") and a carbonate beach on [Long Island, Bahamas](/wiki/Long_Island,_Bahamas "Long Island, Bahamas")

The major areas for deposition of sediments in the marine environment include:

* [Littoral](/wiki/Littoral "Littoral") sands (e.g. beach sands, runoff river sands, coastal bars and spits, largely [clastic](/wiki/Clastic "Clastic") with little faunal content)
* The continental shelf ([silty](/wiki/Silt "Silt") [clays](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), increasing marine faunal content).
* The shelf margin (low terrigenous supply, mostly [calcareous](/wiki/Calcite "Calcite") faunal skeletons)
* The shelf slope (much more fine-grained silts and clays)
* Beds of estuaries with the resultant deposits called "[bay mud](/wiki/Bay_mud "Bay mud")".

One other depositional environment which is a mixture of fluvial and marine is the [turbidite](/wiki/Turbidite "Turbidite") system, which is a major source of sediment to the deep [sedimentary](/wiki/Sedimentary_basin "Sedimentary basin") and [abyssal basins](/wiki/Abyssal_plain "Abyssal plain") as well as the deep [oceanic trenches](/wiki/Oceanic_trench "Oceanic trench").

Any depression in a marine environment where sediments accumulate over time is known as a [sediment trap](/wiki/Sediment_trap_(geology) "Sediment trap (geology)").

The null point theory explains how sediment deposition undergoes a hydrodynamic sorting process within the marine environment leading to a seaward fining of sediment grain size.

See also: [Sediment transport § Applications](/wiki/Sediment_transport#Applications "Sediment transport")

### Erosion and agricultural sediment delivery to rivers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Erosion and agricultural sediment delivery to rivers")]

One cause of high sediment loads is [slash and burn](/wiki/Slash_and_burn "Slash and burn") and [shifting cultivation](/wiki/Shifting_cultivation "Shifting cultivation") of [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") forests. When the ground surface is stripped of vegetation and then seared of all living organisms, the upper soils are vulnerable to both wind and water erosion. In a number of regions of the earth, entire sectors of a country have become erodible. For example, on the [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") high central [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau"), which constitutes approximately ten percent of that country's land area, most of the land area is devegetated, and gullies have eroded into the underlying soil to form distinctive gulleys called *[lavakas](/wiki/Lavaka "Lavaka")*. These are typically 40 meters (130 ft) wide, 80 meters (260 ft) long and 15 meters (49 ft) deep.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Some areas have as many as 150 lavakas/square kilometer,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and lavakas may account for 84% of all sediments carried off by rivers.[[14]](#cite_note-14) This [siltation](/wiki/Siltation "Siltation") results in discoloration of rivers to a dark red brown color and leads to fish kills. In addition, sedimentation of river basins implies sediment management and siltation costs. The cost of removing an estimated 135 million m3 of accumulated sediments due to water erosion only is likely exceeding 2.3 billion euro (€) annually in the EU and UK, with large regional differences between countries.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Erosion is also an issue in areas of modern farming, where the removal of native vegetation for the cultivation and harvesting of a single type of crop has left the soil unsupported.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Many of these regions are near rivers and drainages. Loss of soil due to erosion removes useful farmland, adds to sediment loads, and can help transport anthropogenic fertilizers into the river system, which leads to [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The Sediment Delivery Ratio (SDR) is fraction of gross erosion (interill, rill, gully and stream erosion) that is expected to be delivered to the outlet of the river.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The sediment transfer and deposition can be modelled with sediment distribution models such as WaTEM/SEDEM.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In Europe, according to WaTEM/SEDEM model estimates the Sediment Delivery Ratio is about 15%.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Coastal development and sedimentation near coral reefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Coastal development and sedimentation near coral reefs")]

Watershed development near coral reefs is a primary cause of sediment-related coral stress. The stripping of natural vegetation in the watershed for development exposes soil to increased wind and rainfall and, as a result, can cause exposed sediment to become more susceptible to erosion and delivery to the marine environment during rainfall events. Sediment can negatively affect corals in many ways, such as by physically smothering them, abrading their surfaces, causing corals to expend energy during sediment removal, and causing algal blooms that can ultimately lead to less space on the seafloor where juvenile corals (polyps) can settle.

When sediments are introduced into the coastal regions of the ocean, the proportion of land, marine, and organic-derived sediment that characterizes the seafloor near sources of sediment output is altered. In addition, because the source of sediment (i.e., land, ocean, or organically) is often correlated with how coarse or fine sediment grain sizes that characterize an area are on average, grain size distribution of sediment will shift according to the relative input of land (typically fine), marine (typically coarse), and organically-derived (variable with age) sediment. These alterations in marine sediment characterize the amount of sediment suspended in the water column at any given time and sediment-related coral stress.
[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Biological considerations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sediment&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Biological considerations")]

In July 2020, [marine biologists](/wiki/Marine_biology "Marine biology") reported that [aerobic](/wiki/Aerobic_organism "Aerobic organism") [microorganisms](/wiki/Microorganism "Microorganism") (mainly), in "[quasi-suspended animation](/wiki/Suspended_animation "Suspended animation")", were found in organically-poor sediments, up to 101.5 million years old, 250 feet below the [seafloor](/wiki/Seabed "Seabed") in the [South Pacific Gyre](/wiki/South_Pacific_Gyre "South Pacific Gyre") (SPG) ("the deadest spot in the ocean"), and could be the [longest-living life forms](/wiki/List_of_longest-living_organisms "List of longest-living organisms") ever found.[[22]](#cite_note-NYT-2200728-22)[[23]](#cite_note-NC-20200728-23)
